title: NIS2 Industrial Control System Patching and Change Evidence
exclusions: Does not confirm that ICS/OT patches were tested in a staging environment, evaluate whether the change management process meets IEC 62443 requirements, or replace OT engineering sign-off. Only tracks patch deployment timelines and change-ticket correlation for industrial control systems.
evidence: Weekly saved search `UC-22.2.38`, OT CAB-signed CSV revision, and PDF export stored in the operations evidence library with timestamped archive metadata.

compliance.assurance_rationale: Art.21(2)(e) requires security in acquisition, development and maintenance including vulnerability handling. The UC tracks ICS patch deployment timelines and change-ticket correlation for OT assets. It proves change discipline is evidenced but cannot confirm that the patches were tested in a staging environment before OT deployment, hence partial.
compliance.controlObjective: Evidence that NIS2 Art.21(2)(e) (Security in acquisition, development and maintenance) is enforced — Splunk UC-22.2.38: NIS2 Industrial Control System Patching and Change Evidence.
compliance.evidenceArtifact: Saved search 'UC-22.2.38' running on index=ot_changes (HEC), index=itsm sourcetype="snow:change_request", nis2_ot_patch_policy.csv, archived to the restricted audit_evidence index (default 7-year retention). Auto-drafted — SME review required.

controlTest.positiveScenario: Inject telemetry where `observed_fw` is `2.0.0` while the lookup mandates `2.1.0` and `last_change` epoch precedes `last_approved_change_epoch`; the search returns the asset_id with both noncompliance indicators set.
controlTest.negativeScenario: Send telemetry matching `required_fw_version` with `last_change` newer than the CAB epoch plus fixtures flagged `LAB-` assets filtered out by production macro; the saved search completes with zero production rows.

dataSources: `index=ot_changes` `sourcetype=ot:vendor:patch` or `sourcetype=windecs:changelog` via **HTTP Event Collector** token (port 8088) from PLC engineering workstation agents; `index=itsm` `sourcetype=snow:change_request` (number, start_date, end_date, cmdb_ci) via Splunk Add-on for ServiceNow ([Splunkbase 1928](https://splunkbase.splunk.com/app/1928)); baseline CSV `nis2_ot_patch_policy.csv` (asset_id, required_fw_version, last_approved_change_epoch).
app: Splunk Add-on for ServiceNow ([Splunkbase 1928](https://splunkbase.splunk.com/app/1928)), Splunk Connect for OT / generic HEC for vendor patch forwarders; optional Splunk Add-on for Microsoft SCCM ([Splunkbase 1599](https://splunkbase.splunk.com/app/1599)) for parallel IT plant gateways.
spl:
index=ot_changes earliest=-90d
| stats latest(firmware_version) as observed_fw latest(_time) as last_change by asset_id
| lookup nis2_ot_patch_policy.csv asset_id OUTPUT required_fw_version last_approved_change_epoch
| eval fw_compliant=if(observed_fw=required_fw_version,1,0)
| eval change_stale=if(last_change < last_approved_change_epoch,1,0)
| where fw_compliant=0 OR change_stale=1
| table asset_id, observed_fw, required_fw_version, last_change, change_stale
description: Compares the latest firmware string and last engineering change timestamp observed in OT vendor telemetry against an approved baseline table to find PLCs or RTUs that drifted from policy or never recorded a change after the last CAB approval epoch.
value: Plant managers can hand an inspector a Splunk report that ties patch windows to actual controller versions without walking the shop floor with USB sticks, reducing downtime disputes when a CVE fix was scheduled but not propagated.
implementation: (1) Route Rockwell/Siemens/Schneider engineering tool exports or agent logs to `index=ot_changes` through HEC with TLS. (2) Maintain `nis2_ot_patch_policy.csv` from monthly OT CAB minutes including `last_approved_change_epoch`. (3) Pull `snow:change_request` (1928) for contextual CHG numbers — join in a second panel. (4) Schedule search weekly due to low event rates. (5) Store evidence **7 years** in `audit_evidence` if mandated.
visualization: Table of non-compliant asset_id, timeline of last_change, bar chart of stale changes by plant site.
dataModelAcceleration: Not required for this use case.
knownFalsePositives:
• **Vendor telemetry reports maintenance string not applied yet** — Distinguish: ladder download queued but reboot pending next outage. Suppress: time-bound exception `ot_pending_reboot.csv` with asset_id, expiry.
• **Duplicate asset_id after site rename** — Distinguish: new CSV row supersedes old device. Suppress: lookup `asset_id_alias.csv` mapping retired IDs.
• **Firmware normalization mismatch (01.04 vs 1.4)** — Distinguish: string compare fails though semantically equal. Suppress: `eval observed_norm=replace(observed_fw,"^0+","")` before compare.
• **ServiceNow CAB timestamp in local timezone** — Distinguish: epoch off by 3600s causing change_stale=1. Suppress: convert all epochs to UTC in CSV build script.
• **Test bench floods HEC** — Distinguish: asset_id prefixed `LAB-`. Suppress: `where NOT match(asset_id,"^LAB-")` filter on production dashboards only.
equipmentModels: Rockwell Automation Studio 5000 / FactoryTalk AssetCentre (PLC firmware and project management)
Schneider Electric Unity Pro / EcoStruxure Control Expert (Modicon PLC engineering)
ServiceNow ITSM (change_request table for OT CAB records)
Siemens TIA Portal / STEP 7 (S7 PLC programming and firmware)
Splunk HTTP Event Collector v2 (OT telemetry ingestion)
detailedImplementation:
## Prerequisites

• **Splunk**: Enterprise ≥9.2 or Splunk Cloud (current). Scheduling requires a search head not under memory pressure (check `index=_internal sourcetype=splunkd component=ProcessRunner` for OOM warnings before deploying a new scheduled search).
• **Indexes**: `ot_changes` must exist on all indexers with retention matching the regulation's minimum (PCI: 1y, HIPAA: 6y, NIST 800-53: 1-3y per FIPS 199, SOX: 7y). Set `frozenTimePeriodInSecs` accordingly in `indexes.conf`.
• **Lookups**: `nis2_ot_patch_policy.csv` must be deployed to the search-tier app and refreshed on a documented schedule. For KV Store lookups, verify with `| rest /servicesNS/-/-/data/transforms/lookups | search name IN ('nis2_ot_patch_policy.csv')`.
• **Regulatory mapping**: This detection evidences clause `Art.21(2)(e)`. The control objective is: "Evidence that NIS2 Art.21(2)(e) (Security in acquisition, development and maintenance) is enforced — Splunk UC-22.2.38: NIS2 Industrial Control System Patching and Change Evidence." — every alert payload should include this text so downstream consumers know the compliance purpose.

## Step 1 — Configure data collection

Data collection is bespoke for this UC — no single canonical TA parses every source the detection relies on. Route data into `ot_changes` under the relevant sourcetypes, and document the ingestion path in the UC runbook so future engineers know where each field originates.


6 operational considerations apply to the rollout of this detection:


• **Hint 1:** Route Rockwell/Siemens/Schneider engineering tool exports or agent logs to `index=ot_changes` through HEC with TLS. Report-generation cadence must match the regulatory reporting cycle; a monthly digest satisfies an annual review requirement but not a quarterly one.

• **Hint 2:** Maintain `nis2_ot_patch_policy.csv` from monthly OT CAB minutes including `last_approved_change_epoch`. The lookup table identified here is a long-lived compliance artifact; version it in source control and track every change with a pull-request review.

• **Hint 3:** Pull `snow:change_request`. This is an operational consideration that influences either the detection's accuracy or its maintainability — document the approach chosen in the UC runbook.

• **Hint 4:** for contextual CHG numbers — join in a second panel. This is an operational consideration that influences either the detection's accuracy or its maintainability — document the approach chosen in the UC runbook.

• **Hint 5:** Schedule search weekly due to low event rates. Schedule cadence is a tradeoff between regulatory latency and search-head load; document the chosen cadence and the reasoning in the UC runbook.

• **Hint 6:** Store evidence **7 years** in `audit_evidence` if mandated. This is an operational consideration that influences either the detection's accuracy or its maintainability — document the approach chosen in the UC runbook.

## Step 2 — Implement the search

For "NIS2 Industrial Control System Patching and Change Evidence," the aggregation is the evidence artifact itself — auditors will read these rows as the official record of NIS2 control activity.

This search enriches raw events with context from the lookup `nis2_ot_patch_policy.csv` — the policy register codifying the approved configuration baseline. The raw event stream alone cannot answer the compliance question because it does not carry the authoritative business attributes (`required_fw_version last_approved_change_epoch`) — these live in a register maintained outside the monitored system. The `| lookup` joins at search time on `asset_id`, making the search results only as accurate as the lookup's freshness.

Lookup ownership and freshness: This is the single most common failure mode for lookup-based compliance searches. The `nis2_ot_patch_policy.csv` table must be owned by a named team with a documented refresh cadence and staleness alerting. A minimum operational SLA is: (1) lookup refreshed at a cadence matching the risk domain (daily for access control, weekly for inventory, quarterly for vendor registers), (2) automated alert when the lookup has not been rewritten in 2x the refresh interval, (3) a row-count sanity check (alert if row count changes by more than 20% between refreshes — catches truncation bugs in upstream exports).

Case sensitivity: Splunk's lookup matching is case-sensitive by default. If the raw event field and the lookup key differ in case (e.g. `Account_Name=JSMITH` vs lookup `samaccountname=jsmith`), the join silently returns null. Normalize both sides with `| eval asset_id = lower(asset_id)` before the lookup, or set `case_sensitive_match = false` in the lookup's `transforms.conf` stanza. Search across the last 90 days.

**Schedule configuration** (`savedsearches.conf`):
```ini
[UC-22.2.38: NIS2 Industrial Control System Patching and Change Evidence]
cron_schedule = 0 3 1 * *
dispatch.earliest_time = -90d
dispatch.latest_time = now
actions = summary_index
action.summary_index._name = summary_compliance
action.summary_index.marker = uc=22.2.38,reg=NIS2
```

## Step 3 — Validate deployment

**(a) Field extraction check:** Run the following to confirm the TA is extracting every field the detection relies on:

```spl
index=ot_changes sourcetype=the_sourcetype earliest=-1h
| fieldsummary
| where count=0
| table field
```

Any row returned is a field the TA claims to extract but isn't appearing in recent data — it's either a rename upstream, a TA version mismatch, or a data-format change.

**(b) Required-field presence:** This detection specifically needs: `asset_id`, `change_stale`, `fw_compliant`, `last_change`, `observed_fw`, `required_fw_version`. Run:

```spl
index=ot_changes sourcetype=the_sourcetype earliest=-1h
| head 100
| table asset_id, change_stale, fw_compliant, last_change, observed_fw, required_fw_version
```

Every row should have non-null values for the fields the detection's `where` clause filters on. Nulls cause silent drops in the result set.

**(c) Lookup freshness:** The detection joins against `nis2_ot_patch_policy.csv`. Check its modification time and row count:

```spl
| inputlookup nis2_ot_patch_policy.csv
| eval _lookup_row=1
| stats count as rows
```

Compare the row count to the expected baseline; an unexpected drop is a sign the upstream export truncated.

**(e) Negative test:** Generate a synthetic 'should not alert' event using the Splunk Sample Generator or a crafted `| makeresults` event and confirm the detection does NOT fire on it. A detection that fires on everything is equivalent to a detection that fires on nothing.

## Step 4 — Operationalize (dashboard and reporting)

Create a companion dashboard titled `UC-22.2.38: NIS2 evidence` with the following panel layout:

**Panel 1 — Table Of Non-Compliant Asset Id (the key fields):** Configure according to standard Splunk visualization patterns for this panel type.

**Panel 2 — Timeline Of Last Change (the key fields):** Configure according to standard Splunk visualization patterns for this panel type.

**Panel 3 — Bar Chart Of Stale Changes By Plant Site (the key fields):** Configure according to standard Splunk visualization patterns for this panel type.


Time picker defaults: set to last 30 days with named presets for 24h, 7d, 30d, 90d, and 1y — the auditor needs all five windows without retyping. Enable scheduled PDF export for the full dashboard on the regulation's reporting cadence.

## Step 5 — Troubleshooting and regulation-specific caveats

**No results when violations exist:** Check the lookup's case sensitivity (Splunk lookups are case-sensitive by default) and null handling — a null on the input side produces a silent miss, not an error.

**Search times out:** Check the search job inspector (`Activity > Jobs > <this UC's last run> > Inspect`). The phase consuming the time (fetch, command, summary) indicates whether the issue is data-layer (too wide a window, unaccelerated datamodel), command-layer (expensive `transaction` or `eventstats`), or post-aggregation.


**NIS2-specific failure modes:**

• 24/7 reporting obligation (Art. 23): NIS2 requires early warning within 24 hours of awareness of a significant incident. Detections that back this SLA must compute elapsed time from the SOC's first-acknowledged timestamp (not the resolve-time) and must trigger independently of business hours — the regulatory clock does not pause overnight.

• Member-state variation: NIS2 is transposed individually by each EU Member State with some latitude on thresholds and sector scoping. A detection correct for German IT-SiG 3 (BSI) may be misaligned with Dutch CWV or French LPM. Use `nis2_member_state_config.csv` to parametrize thresholds per jurisdiction; never hardcode.

• Essential vs Important entity criteria: the applicable obligations depend on sector + size. Before alerting, verify the monitored entity's designation; a detection firing on an out-of-scope subsidiary causes unnecessary regulatory notifications that erode trust with the national CSIRT.

• Supply-chain (Art. 21(2)(d)) extent: NIS2 explicitly includes direct suppliers' cybersecurity posture. Detections must cover monitored-by-you-not-owned-by-you infrastructure (outsourced SaaS, managed SIEM tenants) — these are in scope even if operationally run by a third party. BA/BAA-style governance applies.
grandmaExplanation: We check factory controllers against the approved version list and the official maintenance schedule, so you can prove the sensitive equipment got its fixes on time and nothing silently drifted out of spec.
